      SPAC SPAC SPAC all rightEvery time I write about special purpose acqu=
isition companies someone emails me to be like =E2=80=9CI am going to launc=
h a SPAC of SPACs,=E2=80=9D and it is always pretty=C2=A0half-baked. A SPAC=
 is a blank-check company that raises money to merge with another company, =
and that is a meta enough idea that people want to make it more meta. =E2=
=80=9CA SPAC that raises money to buy SPACs=E2=80=9D=C2=A0is=C2=A0more meta=
, sure, but it doesn=E2=80=99t make any sense. A SPAC is a tool, it=E2=80=
=99s a particular way to raise money to take companies public; multiplying =
the abstraction doesn=E2=80=99t add anything.=C2=A0But  here=E2=80=99s this=
:It=E2=80=99s the latest twist in the world of blank-check mergers: A compa=
ny plans to go public with a SPAC and use it to buy back an affiliate that =
it took public using another SPAC.This circular scenario revolves around a =
drugmaker called=C2=A0Roivant Sciences Ltd., which wants to merge with a sp=
ecial-purpose acquisition company and then take over a SPAC that acquired=
=C2=A0Immunovant Inc.=C2=A0from Roivant less than two years ago.What=E2=80=
=99s more, Roivant says it knows something that everyone else doesn=E2=80=
=99t about its former unit, and it=E2=80=99s willing to pay a premium for t=
he shares -- perhaps as much as 70% by one estimate.A SPAC-on-SPAC deal is =
such an oddity that people who follow shell companies can=E2=80=99t remembe=
r it happening before, or anything like Roivant=E2=80=99s head-spinning ver=
sion. =E2=80=9CIn the years I=E2=80=99ve been analyzing SPACs, I=E2=80=99ve=
 never seen a transaction like this,=E2=80=9D said=C2=A0Neil Danics, founde=
r of SPAC Analytics in Toronto and who has been providing research on the i=
ndustry since 2007. =E2=80=A6=E2=80=9CIf you can raise a bunch of money wit=
h a SPAC and then use that relatively cheap cash to buy a company at a valu=
ation they find attractive -- that seems like a rational approach,=E2=80=9D=
 says Nikolai Roussanov, a finance professor at the University of Pennsylva=
nia=E2=80=99s Wharton School.The plan goes like this: Roivant has proposed =
to be acquired by a SPAC called Montes Archimedes Acquisition Corp. in a co=
mbination valued at about $7.3 billion, according to filings. Roivant would=
 be the surviving entity, and in turn, would offer a mix of stock and cash =
to buy up shares that it doesn=E2=80=99t already own in Immunovant, an earl=
y-stage drugmaker. Roivant spun that off in 2019 into a SPAC called Health =
Sciences Acquisitions Corp., in return for shares and other considerations =
initially valued at $395 million.The reason Roivant is willing to pay up is=
n=E2=80=99t immediately clear. Human trials on Immunovant=E2=80=99s main pr=
ospect, a monoclonal antibody injection aimed at ailments such as myastheni=
a gravis and thyroid eye disease, were halted February 2 because of concern=
 about potential side effects.The SPAC-on-SPAC angle is funny, but I am not=
 sure one should overemphasize it. Nothing really turns on any of this bein=
g a SPAC. The story is that Roivant is a company that operates as a sort of=
 pharmaceutical=C2=A0conglomerate/incubator. It says:We develop drug candid=
ates in subsidiary companies we call =E2=80=9CVants=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6.=C2=
=A0Our Vants continue to benefit from the support of the Roivant platform a=
nd technologies that are built to address inefficiencies in the drug discov=
ery, development and commercialization process. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0The Vant mod=
el also enables a modular approach to the monetization of therapies we adva=
nce through development, allowing us to pursue commercialization of some pr=
oducts independently, while selectively establishing partnerships for other=
 Vants or divesting of the Vants entirely.Since our founding in 2014, we ha=
ve =E2=80=A6=C2=A0launched and taken public multiple Vants, resulting in an=
 aggregate ownership stake of $1.1 billion in public Vants as of April 30, =
2021, based on a $288 million aggregate investment in those Vants.So it dev=
elops drugs in subsidiaries and takes some of those subsidiaries public, wh=
ile retaining=C2=A0majority stakes in them. Once upon a time it might have =
done that with initial public offerings, but now obviously the way to take =
a pre-revenue=C2=A0drug company public is by merging it with a SPAC, so tha=
t=E2=80=99s what Roivant did with=C2=A0Immunovant=C2=A0in late 2019.Now Imm=
unovant is public, Roivant owns 57.5% of it, and apparently Roivant knows s=
omething that the market does not: =E2=80=9CAs the Issuer=E2=80=99s control=
ling shareholder, Roivant has received nonpublic information about the Issu=
er and its lead product candidate,=E2=80=9D says Roivant=E2=80=99s Schedule=
 13D. So it wants to buy up the Immunovant shares that it doesn=E2=80=99t o=
wn, at a premium to their market price. That will require money, perhaps a =
billion dollars or so, which means going out to investors to raise the mone=
y.[1] In today=E2=80=99s market, if you are a pharmaceutical company going =
to investors to raise money, you might as well go to a SPAC: SPACs have lot=
s of money and are looking to spend it.=C2=A0Obviously IPOing a pre-revenue=
 drug company and then buying it back a year later because you know somethi=
ng about its drug candidate that the market doesn=E2=80=99t is itself an in=
teresting business move! But doing both legs of the deal =E2=80=94=C2=A0IPO=
ing the company and then buying it back =E2=80=94=C2=A0via SPAC is not=C2=
=A0especially=C2=A0strange. It=E2=80=99s just that SPACs are the hottest al=
l-purpose tool of corporate finance right now, or at least they were a few =
months ago when all this was dreamed up. If you want to raise money to sell=
 a company, or to buy it back, why not do it with a SPAC?Bitcoin went downH=
mm,  well:Bitcoin took investors on a wild ride Wednesday, plunging as much=
 as 29% before cutting the drop in half in a bout of selling that drew atte=
ntion across Wall Street and social media. ...Bitcoin plunged as much as 29=
%, wiping out more than $500 billion in value from the coin=E2=80=99s peak =
market value. It was down 15% to $36,700 as of 10:53 a.m. in New York. It h=
as erased all the gains it clocked up following Tesla Inc.=E2=80=99s Feb. 8=
 announcement that it would use corporate cash to buy the asset and accept =
it as a form of payment for its vehicles. Ethereum, the second-biggest coin=
, sank more than 40%, while joke token Dogecoin lost 45%. ...Tesla CEO Elon=
 Musk sparked the wild moves last week. Bitcoin plunged when he announced t=
he carmaker wouldn=E2=80=99t take it as a payment, but then reversed when h=
e said the company had no plans to sell its corporate crypto holdings. He s=
eemed to imply in a tweet Wednesday that Tesla is not selling into the rout=
.Yeah the way he implied that is by tweeting =E2=80=9CTesla has diamond han=
ds,=E2=80=9D only with a diamond emoji and a hands emoji instead of the wor=
ds =E2=80=9Cdiamond=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Chands.=E2=80=9D It would be funny=
 if Tesla was dumping all of its Bitcoins while he was tweeting that. Would=
 it be fraud? Imagine being the Securities and Exchange Commission and tryi=
ng to bring that case.[2] =E2=80=9CYour Honor, clearly the diamond emoji fo=
llowed by the hands emoji meant that Tesla was not selling any of its Bitco=
in holdings, and market participants would have interpreted it that way.=E2=
=80=9D Absolutely true! Yet somehow embarrassing to say.=C2=A0Anyway Musk t=
weeted that at 10:42 a.m. New York time, and in the following half hour Bit=
coin rallied from a bit under $35,000 to a bit over $37,000. He=E2=80=99s s=
till got it!  I wrote, ugh, Monday that Musk has the ability to make Bitcoi=
n go up or down whenever he wants, with his tweets, with no limits on that =
power. And today there=E2=80=99s a huge Bitcoin crash =E2=80=94=C2=A0caused=
 in part by Musk=E2=80=99s bad-for-Bitcoin=C2=A0tweeting=C2=A0over the week=
end, though also by a Chinese regulatory crackdown=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0and =
so Musk decided to make Bitcoin go back up.=C2=A0So he tweeted two words an=
d two emojis and, bang, it went back up.=C2=A0The best estimate  I=E2=80=99=
ve seen for the price that Tesla paid for its Bitcoins is about $34,700, an=
d it has about $1.3 billion of Bitcoins at that price. Bitcoin briefly got =
as low as $30,000 this morning, which would put=C2=A0Tesla down about $180=
=C2=A0million on its Bitcoin stash =E2=80=94=C2=A0which (unlike any unreali=
zed gains) Tesla would have  to report as a loss in its quarterly=C2=A0earn=
ings. It would be kind of=C2=A0weird if Tesla had to report=C2=A0a nine-fig=
ure loss this quarter caused primarily by its CEO trash-talking one of its =
assets. Normal CEOs don=E2=80=99t do that. Fortunately Tesla doesn=E2=80=99=
t have to report a loss like that, because if the price of Bitcoin ever get=
s below its cost basis, Musk can just make the price go back up again! Like=
 he just did.Elsewhere, the great thing about decentralized trustless block=
chain-based digital assets is that when the price goes down  no one can tra=
de them:The world=E2=80=99s biggest crypto-trading platforms were hit by di=
sruptions as digital currencies from Bitcoin to Ethereum slid in a collapse=
 that wiped out billions of dollars in market value.Binance, the world=E2=
=80=99s biggest cryptocurrency exchange, temporarily disabled Ethereum with=
drawals, citing network congestion, while Coinbase Global Inc. reported =E2=
=80=9Cintermittent downtime=E2=80=9D on its platform, before saying it had =
identified and fixed the issue. Kraken said it=E2=80=99s working to fix som=
e client disruptions.

DiscoveryDiscovery Inc. has three classes of stock. There are 162.5 million=
 shares of Series A stock (ticker DISCA), which has one vote per share. The=
re are 6.5 million shares of Series B stock (DISCB), which has 10 votes per=
 share. There are 318.3 million shares of Series C stock (DISCK), which has=
 no votes.=C2=A0How much more would you pay for voting Series A stock than =
you=E2=80=99d pay for non-voting Series C stock? I dunno; I am not all that=
 convinced that voting rights in a public company are particularly valuable=
. But there is a market answer to this question. Yesterday the Series A=C2=
=A0closed at $33.31 per share, while the Series C closed at $31.29, for a s=
pread of $2.02. The median spread in 2021 so far has been $6.10; it got as =
high as $13.45 on March 16, while yesterday=E2=80=99s $2.02=C2=A0was its lo=
w point for the year.=C2=A0How much more would you pay for super-voting Ser=
ies B stock than you=E2=80=99d pay for regular-voting Series A stock? Super=
 votes are nice, but generally not as nice as they sound. John C. Malone, t=
he cable-TV mogul and financial-structuring legend who once ran a predecess=
or of Discovery and is currently on its board of directors, owns 95% of the=
 Series B stock.=C2=A0He=C2=A0has super-voting power, sure; he owns somethi=
ng like 4% of the company but has 26.5% of the votes. But if=C2=A0you=C2=A0=
buy 1,000 shares of Series B stock, that will not give you much say over th=
e company. In fact, if you buy all 327,099 Series B shares that Malone does=
n=E2=80=99t own, that will give you only=C2=A03.27 million votes, or about =
1.4% of the voting power of Discovery=E2=80=99s shares.=C2=A0Super-voting s=
tock is generally a way to concentrate control in the hands of one or two i=
nfluential founder types (here, Malone); it is not generally a way for, lik=
e, an activist hedge fund to efficiently accumulate a lot of voting power i=
n the open market.=C2=A0Also, as is often true of super-voting shares, the =
Series B trades much less actively than the Series A (or Series C), so you =
might expect it to have an illiquidity discount. It=E2=80=99s easier to sel=
l a lot of Series A stock quickly, which you might find valuable.Nonetheles=
s yesterday the Series A closed at $33.31 per share, while the Series B clo=
sed at $78, for a premium of $44.69. In 2021 so far, the spread between the=
m has been as high as $84.54 =E2=80=94=C2=A0on March 31, when the Series B =
closed at $128 and the Series A at $43.46 =E2=80=94=C2=A0and as low as nega=
tive $1.52 in January.It=E2=80=99s weird. One rationale you might have had=
=C2=A0for paying up for the Series B might have gone like this: If I buy Se=
ries B stock, I will not have much power over Discovery. But John Malone ow=
ns a lot of Series B stock and has a lot of power over Discovery, including=
 some power to push or block a merger. Since he has this power, he will wan=
t to get a premium for his Series B shares in any merger. Therefore, if Dis=
covery agrees to a merger, my Series B stock will get paid off much more th=
an Series A stock will, so I should pay more for it now.I don=E2=80=99t kno=
w if anyone had this rationale, but if they did it was wrong. Discovery=C2=
=A0did=C2=A0agree to a merger: On Monday, AT&T Inc. announced that it would=
 spin off its WarnerMedia content assets and combine them with Discovery in=
 =E2=80=9Can all-stock, Reverse Morris Trust transaction.=E2=80=9D The new =
combined company will have one class of stock, and every Discovery share =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0Series A, B and C =E2=80=94=C2=A0will be exchanged for one u=
ndifferentiated share of the new company.[3] Malone, in particular, is  not=
 getting anything extra:=E2=80=9CI am delighted to fully support this trans=
action, without asking for or receiving a premium for my high vote shares,=
=E2=80=9D Malone said Tuesday in a statement. =E2=80=9CI believe we are cre=
ating real value for shareholders and a legacy investment for my grandkids.=
=E2=80=9DThat=E2=80=99s from an article titled =E2=80=9CCable Billionaire M=
alone May Take $280 Million Hit on AT&T-Discovery Deal.=E2=80=9D That $280 =
million is just his 6.2 million high-vote shares times the $44.69 premium a=
t which they traded yesterday; that premium will go away when the merger cl=
oses.Why are the Series B shares=C2=A0still trading at a premium? I suppose=
 the main answer is just that they don=E2=80=99t trade that much; only abou=
t 17,000 shares traded yesterday, and only about 44,000 traded on Monday wh=
en the deal was announced. (On Monday, 89.5 million shares of Series A and =
37.5 million shares of Series C traded.) Only so many people have Series B =
shares and want to sell them, and because they are so concentrated they are=
 hard to borrow, so no one is really shorting them. There is not really an =
arbitrage. Still, somebody=C2=A0is selling, a little bit, which makes sense=
; you might as well cash in that premium now while it still exists. The que=
stion is why=C2=A0anyone wants to=C2=A0buy=C2=A0them.In other Discovery/AT&=
T news, here=E2=80=99s how mergers happen in 2021:AT&T=E2=80=99s negotiatio=
ns with Discovery heated up earlier this year, when Discovery Chief Executi=
ve David Zaslav texted [AT&T CEO John]=C2=A0Stankey a series of emojis duri=
ng the airing of the Pro-Am golf tournament at Pebble Beach, which AT&T spo=
nsors. Talks continued with face-to-face meetings at Mr. Zaslav=E2=80=99s G=
reenwich Village townhouse in New York City and meals outdoors.What were th=
e emojis? Is there an emoji that is generally understood to mean =E2=80=9Cm=
erger=E2=80=9D? Eventually AT&T and Discovery will file a registration stat=
ement and proxy for the merger, and there will be a =E2=80=9CBackground of =
the Merger=E2=80=9D section with an extensive play-by-play of how the deal =
was conceived and negotiated. Surely this anecdote will be in the backgroun=
d section. Will it be bland and vague=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Con Feb. =
11, 2014, Mr. Zaslav contacted Mr. Stankey to propose further discussions a=
bout a business combination,=E2=80=9D etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0or will it list t=
he emojis? Will it print the emojis, or describe them in words? =E2=80=9CMr=
. Zaslav sent Mr. Stankey a text message consisting of the =E2=80=98emojis=
=E2=80=99 for man swinging golf club, hand with thumb up, and=C2=A0smiling =
face, followed by another text message consisting of the =E2=80=98emojis=E2=
=80=99 for=C2=A0bag with dollar sign on it, bag with dollar sign on it, bag=
 with dollar sign on it, bag with dollar sign on it,=C2=A0bag with dollar s=
ign on it,=C2=A0bag with dollar sign on it,=C2=A0bag with dollar sign on it=
, telephone, question mark.=E2=80=9DElsewhere in M&AA good general rule of =
thumb is that if you hire a specialist to invest in an=C2=A0asset class, th=
at person will be good at choosing which particular assets in that class to=
 buy, but bad at deciding how much money to allocate to that asset class ov=
erall. If you hire a tech-stocks analyst, she will (you hope) be good at pi=
cking which tech stocks will outperform other tech stocks. But if the tech =
sector as a whole is overvalued, she probably will not come to you and say =
=E2=80=9Cyou should stop investing in tech stocks.=E2=80=9D Her job is inve=
sting in tech stocks, and the more tech stocks you buy the more important (=
and better paid) she is, so her bias is to get you to buy more tech stocks.=
 If she gets you to buy the (relatively) correct tech stocks, but tech stoc=
ks as a whole underperform, you may be worse off than if you had never hire=
d a tech specialist.Similarly, some public companies have mergers-and-acqui=
sitions departments, and others don=E2=80=99t. The trade-off is pretty much=
:=09If you have an M&A department, the people in it might do a good job of =
deciding which acquisitions to pursue, how much to pay for them and how to =
integrate them, but they will want to do a lot of M&A.=09If you don=E2=80=
=99t have an M&A department, you=E2=80=99ll choose your acquisitions somewh=
at haphazardly, but you might do=C2=A0fewer=C2=A0of them.One theory of M&A =
is that acquisitions are=C2=A0corporate finance decisions like any other, a=
nd that you should try to do ones with positive expected value and avoid on=
es with negative value. Another theory of M&A is that acquisitions are larg=
ely a way for chief executive officers to =E2=80=9Cempire build,=E2=80=9D a=
ggrandizing themselves by making their companies bigger even at the cost of=
 shareholder value. If you believe the first theory, you might like an M&A =
department, because it might choose good deals and avoid bad ones. If you b=
elieve the second theory, you might hate an M&A department, because it migh=
t just increase the number of deals and encourage the CEO=E2=80=99s empire-=
building.Anyway here's =E2=80=9CDo Firms with Specialized M&A Staff Make Be=
tter Acquisitions?=E2=80=9D by=C2=A0Sinan Gokkaya, Xi Liu and=C2=A0Ren=C3=
=A9=C2=A0Stulz (free SSRN version here):We open the black box of the M&A de=
cision process by constructing a comprehensive sample of US firms with spec=
ialized M&A staff. We investigate whether specialized M&A staff improves ac=
quisition performance or facilitates managerial empire building instead. We=
 find that firms with specialized M&A staff make better acquisitions when a=
cquisition performance is measured by stock price reactions to announcement=
s, long-run stock returns, operating performance, divestitures, and analyst=
 earnings forecasts. This effect does not hold when the CEO is powerful, ov=
erconfident, or entrenched. Acquisitions by firms without specialized staff=
 do not create value, on average. We provide evidence on mechanisms through=
 which specialized M&A staff improves acquisition performance. For identifi=
cation, we use the staggered recognition of inevitable disclosure doctrine =
as a source of exogenous variation in the employment of specialized M&A sta=
ff.So it=E2=80=99s a bit of both, but mostly M&A departments are good. Also=
, the reason M&A departments are good is mostly that they are good at picki=
ng targets, not that they are great at negotiating prices =E2=80=94=C2=A0th=
ough they=C2=A0do=C2=A0seem to be good at keeping down the costs of investm=
ent-bank advice:How does specialized M&A staff create value for the acquire=
r=E2=80=99s shareholders? Our results suggest that specialized M&A staff he=
lps acquirers identify targets that have higher synergies with the acquirer=
 as reflected in higher combined announcement returns and improvements in t=
he combined firm=E2=80=99s abnormal operating performance in the post-acqui=
sition period. We do not find evidence that specialized M&A staff drives a =
better bargain for the acquirer in that such acquirers capture more of the =
combined synergy gains or pay lower takeover premiums. Moreover, while thes=
e firms do not seem to retain fewer external advisors, we find that they pa=
y lower advisory fees. The lower fees paid by firms with specialized M&A st=
aff further suggest that the specialized M&A staff performs some tasks that=
 otherwise would be performed by the investment bankers.=C2=A0That last par=
t is not obvious: Maybe if you hire an M&A staff, they will talk to=C2=A0in=
vestment bankers all day, get used to relying on bankers to do lots of work=
 for them, and feel a sense of fondness and obligation to the bankers that =
leads to higher fees, while if the CEO just cuts her own deals she will not=
 feel particularly beholden to bankers. But, no, empirically, corporate M&A=
 departments seem to be substitutes for investment bankers, not complements=
.=C2=A0Shiba InuWell  there=E2=80=99s that answered:Vitalik Buterin, invent=
or of the Ethereum blockchain, said he won=E2=80=99t get a tax write-off af=
ter donating more than 50 trillion of the joke coin Shiba Inu to the India =
Covid Crypto Relief Fund.Because Shiba Inu, a meta play on the ridiculousne=
ss of Doge Coin, has been incredibly volatile lately, the value of Buterin=
=E2=80=99s donation has fluctuated wildly. At one point it was worth about =
$2 billion, then fell to around $800 million.Buterin said in an interview t=
hat he=E2=80=99s taxed as a citizen of Singapore, whose rules are =E2=80=9C=
significantly cleaner than in the U.S. or Canada.=E2=80=9D He=E2=80=99s not=
 eligible for a write-off, he said.=C2=A0I didn=E2=80=99t really think he w=
ould get a write-off =E2=80=94=C2=A0jurisdiction aside, does he have $1 bil=
lion of taxable income? =E2=80=94=C2=A0but I did  mention the possibility t=
he other day.By the way, when we discussed it, I mentioned that I assumed h=
is basis in the Shiba Inu token=C2=A0was very low or zero =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
=E2=80=9Cpresumably Buterin didn=E2=80=99t pay very much for it,=E2=80=9D I=
 said. That was, uh, an understatement. The way Buterin got 50 trillion Shi=
ba Inu coins was that the developers of Shiba Inu sent them to him, for fre=
e, unasked-for. From the =E2=80=94=C2=A0I'm sorry =E2=80=94=C2=A0Shiba Inu =
woof paper:After we finished minting 1 quadrillion tokens, we put half into=
 Uniswap and threw away the keys. Then, the remaining 50% was =E2=80=9Cburn=
ed=E2=80=9D to Vitalik Buterin=E2=80=99s wallet, which was the start of our=
 Army.A footnote adds:Please note that the Shib in VB=E2=80=99s wallet, at =
the time of this writing, was his #1 HIGHEST valued token, even exceeding t=
he value of the Ethereum there. This is an incredible accomplishment, and w=
e are just getting started.As I understand it, inventors of altcoins someti=
mes =E2=80=9Cburn=E2=80=9D a chunk of those coins to Buterin as a=C2=A0sort=
 of publicity play. You get to say that Vitalik Buterin owns some of your c=
oin. You get to inflate the =E2=80=9Cmarket cap=E2=80=9D of your coin: Mark=
et cap is the trading price times the amount outstanding, and if you put tr=
illions of coins in the hands of someone who won=E2=80=99t sell or spend th=
em, you can inflate the multiplier and achieve a huge market cap without mu=
ch trading. And it is assumed by altcoin developers that Buterin=C2=A0won=
=E2=80=99t=C2=A0sell the altcoins that get =E2=80=9Cburned=E2=80=9D to him,=
 because that would undermine his status as a pure-hearted evangelist for E=
thereum, and because he just seems to be too busy to spend any money.I am s=
ure that Buterin finds it a bit annoying to receive billions of dollars=E2=
=80=99=C2=A0worth of random altcoins? A few conclusions:=09I cannot believe=
 that I typed that sentence; imagine being a person who is annoyed=C2=A0to =
wake up and find yourself a billion dollars richer. Cryptocurrency economic=
s are so weird.=09Feel free to burn half of your billion-dollar altcoins to=
 me instead I guess?=C2=A0=09Buterin=E2=80=99s way of getting around this p=
roblem =E2=80=94=C2=A0donating=C2=A0the random altcoins to a worthy cause, =
and letting the charity sell them =E2=80=94=C2=A0is pretty neat.=09That sai=
d, it doesn=E2=80=99t exactly solve the underlying problem (the problem of =
people inventing random altcoins and sending him a bunch), because this don=
ation seems to have been free good publicity for Shiba Inu, even if it did =
push down the price. Shiba Inu coin is somehow providing hundreds of millio=
ns of dollars of Covid relief in India; seems good.Accounting rapHere=E2=80=
=99s an accounting rap. You presumably know how I feel about this. (Bad, I =
feel bad about this, never do a corporate rap parody.)Things happenAndrea O=
rcel and Ana Bot=C3=ADn face off in Madrid courtroom over pay row.=C2=A0Ban=
k of America to Raise Minimum Wage to  $25 Per Hour in Race for Talent. Pos=
t-Archegos  Hedge Fund Financing Faces Close Scrutiny by BOE. Hertz, the Or=
iginal Meme Stock, Rewards Its True Believers. Colonial Pipeline=E2=80=99s =
Computer Network Temporarily  Goes Dark. China Mobile to List in Shanghai a=
s It Departs NYSE. Hedge Funds Increase Bets on Private Companies. Nvidia t=
ries to stop its chips being used for crypto mining.=C2=A0Newly Launched Bi=
tcoin Pizza Brand Doesn=E2=80=99t Accept Bitcoin.If you'd like to get=C2=A0=
Money=C2=A0Stuff=C2=A0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please=C2=
=A0subscribe at this link. Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other gr=
eat Bloomberg newsletters here. Thanks![1] To be clear, it will also requir=
e *disclosure*: If Roivant and Immunovant agree to a deal, they will need t=
o disclose to Immunovant=E2=80=99s other shareholders any material nonpubli=
c information that Roivant considered in proposing the deal. There will pro=
bably always be some information disparity between Roivant =E2=80=94=C2=A0a=
 drug company that started Immunovant and is the majority shareholder =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0and the Immunovant public shareholders, but if there is big mat=
erial news then they=E2=80=99ll have to disclose it before closing the deal=
.[2] Bitcoin is not a security and it would have to be a case about Tesla s=
tock, which seems tricky, but never mind, never mind.[3] This is discussed =
on=C2=A0page 13 of the investor call transcript.

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; padding-bottom: 5px; margin-top=
: 30px; margin-bottom: 0;">SPAC SPAC SPAC all right</h2><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">Every time I write about special purpose acquisition companies so=
meone emails me to be like =E2=80=9CI am going to launch a SPAC of SPACs,=
=E2=80=9D and it is always pretty=C2=A0half-baked. A SPAC is a blank-check =
company that raises money to merge with another company, and that is a meta=
 enough idea that people want to make it more meta. =E2=80=9CA SPAC that ra=
ises money to buy SPACs=E2=80=9D=C2=A0<em>is=C2=A0</em>more meta, sure, but=
 it doesn=E2=80=99t make any sense. A SPAC is a tool, it=E2=80=99s a partic=
ular way to raise money to take companies public; multiplying the abstracti=
on doesn=E2=80=99t add anything.=C2=A0But <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=
mbergbusiness.com/click/23893912.274019/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9=
uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDUtMTkvYS1zcGFjLXdpbGwtYnV5LWJhY2staXRzLW93bi1zcG=
FjLWFuZC1wYXktYS1zdGFnZ2VyaW5nLXByZW1pdW0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBacc729b1=
" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; t=
ext-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-co=
lor: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> here=E2=
=80=99s this</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s =
the latest twist in the world of blank-check mergers: A company plans to go=
 public with a SPAC and use it to buy back an affiliate that it took public=
 using another SPAC.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This circular scenario=
 revolves around a drugmaker called=C2=A0Roivant Sciences Ltd., which wants=
 to merge with a special-purpose acquisition company and then take over a S=
PAC that acquired=C2=A0Immunovant Inc.=C2=A0from Roivant less than two year=
s ago.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What=E2=80=99s more, Roivant says it=
 knows something that everyone else doesn=E2=80=99t about its former unit, =
and it=E2=80=99s willing to pay a premium for the shares -- perhaps as much=
 as 70% by one estimate.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A SPAC-on-SPAC dea=
l is such an oddity that people who follow shell companies can=E2=80=99t re=
member it happening before, or anything like Roivant=E2=80=99s head-spinnin=
g version. =E2=80=9CIn the years I=E2=80=99ve been analyzing SPACs, I=E2=80=
=99ve never seen a transaction like this,=E2=80=9D said=C2=A0Neil Danics, f=
ounder of SPAC Analytics in Toronto and who has been providing research on =
the industry since 2007. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=
=9CIf you can raise a bunch of money with a SPAC and then use that relative=
ly cheap cash to buy a company at a valuation they find attractive -- that =
seems like a rational approach,=E2=80=9D says Nikolai Roussanov, a finance =
professor at the University of Pennsylvania=E2=80=99s Wharton School.</p><p=
 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The plan goes like this: Roivant has proposed to=
 be acquired by a SPAC called Montes Archimedes Acquisition Corp. in a comb=
ination valued at about $7.3 billion, according to filings. Roivant would b=
e the surviving entity, and in turn, would offer a mix of stock and cash to=
 buy up shares that it doesn=E2=80=99t already own in Immunovant, an early-=
stage drugmaker. Roivant spun that off in 2019 into a SPAC called Health Sc=
iences Acquisitions Corp., in return for shares and other considerations in=
itially valued at $395 million.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The reason =
Roivant is willing to pay up isn=E2=80=99t immediately clear. Human trials =
on Immunovant=E2=80=99s main prospect, a monoclonal antibody injection aime=
d at ailments such as myasthenia gravis and thyroid eye disease, were halte=
d February 2 because of concern about potential side effects.</p></blockquo=
te><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The SPAC-on-SPAC angle is funny, but I am n=
ot sure one should overemphasize it. Nothing really turns on any of this be=
ing a SPAC. The story is that Roivant is a company that operates as a sort =
of pharmaceutical=C2=A0conglomerate/incubator. <a href=3D"https://link.mail=
.bloombergbusiness.com/click/23893912.274019/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9Bcm=
NoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE2MzUwODgvMDAwMTE5MzEyNTIxMTYyMTkxL2Q2NTA2MmRzNC5od=
G0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3069d9b3" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"i=
temscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border=
-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: soli=
d; background-color: #ccc;">It says</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">We develop drug candidates in subsidiary companies we call =E2=80=
=9CVants=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6.=C2=A0Our Vants continue to benefit from the su=
pport of the Roivant platform and technologies that are built to address in=
efficiencies in the drug discovery, development and commercialization proce=
ss. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Vant model also ena=
bles a modular approach to the monetization of therapies we advance through=
 development, allowing us to pursue commercialization of some products inde=
pendently, while selectively establishing partnerships for other Vants or d=
ivesting of the Vants entirely.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Since our f=
ounding in 2014, we have =E2=80=A6=C2=A0launched and taken public multiple =
Vants, resulting in an aggregate ownership stake of $1.1 billion in public =
Vants as of April 30, 2021, based on a $288 million aggregate investment in=
 those Vants.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So it develops d=
rugs in subsidiaries and takes some of those subsidiaries public, while ret=
aining=C2=A0majority stakes in them. Once upon a time it might have done th=
at with initial public offerings, but now obviously the way to take a pre-r=
evenue=C2=A0drug company public is by merging it with a SPAC, so that=E2=80=
=99s what Roivant <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=
23893912.274019/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE3NjQ=
wMTMvMDAwMTE5MzEyNTE5MzIxMTQ3L2Q4MzkzODJkOGsuaHRt/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB=
9fabc1a4" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
did</a> with=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=
23893912.274019/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE3NjQ=
wMTMvMDAwMTIxMzkwMDE5MDI0OTM2L2RlZm0xNGFfaGVhbHRoc2NpZW5jZXMuaHRt/607f07cee=
f4b8524a319a23cB8c4c6202" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" st=
yle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-widt=
h: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgroun=
d-color: #ccc;">Immunovant</a>=C2=A0in late 2019.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">Now Immunovant is public, Roivant owns 57.5% of it, and apparently R=
oivant knows something that the market does not: =E2=80=9CAs the Issuer=E2=
=80=99s controlling shareholder, Roivant has received nonpublic information=
 about the Issuer and its lead product candidate,=E2=80=9D says Roivant=E2=
=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/23893912.2=
74019/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE2MzUwODgvMDAwM=
TEwNDY1OTIxMDMyOTY3L3RtMjE4OTgyZDFfc2MxM2RhLmh0bQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB=
136dbf8b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
Schedule 13D</a>. So it wants to buy up the Immunovant shares that it doesn=
=E2=80=99t own, at a premium to their market price. That will require money=
, perhaps a billion dollars or so, which means going out to investors to ra=
ise the money.[1] In today=E2=80=99s market, if you are a pharmaceutical co=
mpany going to investors to raise money, you might as well go to a SPAC: SP=
ACs have lots of money and are looking to spend it.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"ma=
rgin: 16px 0;">Obviously IPOing a pre-revenue drug company and then buying =
it back a year later because you know something about its drug candidate th=
at the market doesn=E2=80=99t is itself an interesting business move! But d=
oing both legs of the deal =E2=80=94=C2=A0IPOing the company and then buyin=
g it back =E2=80=94=C2=A0via SPAC is not=C2=A0<em>especially=C2=A0</em>stra=
nge. It=E2=80=99s just that SPACs are the hottest all-purpose tool of corpo=
rate finance right now, or at least they were a few months ago when all thi=
s was dreamed up. If you want to raise money to sell a company, or to buy i=
t back, why not do it with a SPAC?</p><h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-=
family: "Arial Black", Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spaci=

, clearly the diamond emoji followed by the hands emoji meant that Tesla wa=
s not selling any of its Bitcoin holdings, and market participants would ha=
ve interpreted it that way.=E2=80=9D Absolutely true! Yet somehow embarrass=
ing to say.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway Musk tweeted that =
at 10:42 a.m. New York time, and in the following half hour Bitcoin rallied=

 dotted; padding-bottom: 5px; margin-top: 30px; margin-bottom: 0;">Elsewher=
e in M&A</h2><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A good general rule of thumb =
is that if you hire a specialist to invest in an=C2=A0asset class, that per=
son will be good at choosing which particular assets in that class to buy, =
but bad at deciding how much money to allocate to that asset class overall.=
 If you hire a tech-stocks analyst, she will (you hope) be good at picking =
which tech stocks will outperform other tech stocks. But if the tech sector=
 as a whole is overvalued, she probably will not come to you and say =E2=80=
=9Cyou should stop investing in tech stocks.=E2=80=9D Her job is investing =
in tech stocks, and the more tech stocks you buy the more important (and be=
tter paid) she is, so her bias is to get you to buy more tech stocks. If sh=
e gets you to buy the (relatively) correct tech stocks, but tech stocks as =
a whole underperform, you may be worse off than if you had never hired a te=
ch specialist.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Similarly, some public compa=
nies have mergers-and-acquisitions departments, and others don=E2=80=99t. T=
he trade-off is pretty much:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padd=
ing-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">If yo=
u have an M&A department, the people in it might do a good job of decid=
ing which acquisitions to pursue, how much to pay for them and how to integ=
rate them, but they will want to do a lot of M&A.</li>=09<li style=3D"m=
argin-bottom: 5px;">If you don=E2=80=99t have an M&A department, you=E2=
=80=99ll choose your acquisitions somewhat haphazardly, but you might do=C2=
=A0<em>fewer=C2=A0</em>of them.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One t=
heory of M&A is that acquisitions are=C2=A0corporate finance decisions =
like any other, and that you should try to do ones with positive expected v=
alue and avoid ones with negative value. Another theory of M&A is that =

ire build,=E2=80=9D aggrandizing themselves by making their companies bigge=
r even at the cost of shareholder value. If you believe the first theory, y=
ou might like an M&A department, because it might choose good deals and=

A department, because it might just increase the number of deals and encour=

><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We open the black box of the M&A decision=
 process by constructing a comprehensive sample of US firms with specialize=
d M&A staff. We investigate whether specialized M&A staff improves =
acquisition performance or facilitates managerial empire building instead. =
We find that firms with specialized M&A staff make better acquisitions =
when acquisition performance is measured by stock price reactions to announ=
cements, long-run stock returns, operating performance, divestitures, and a=
nalyst earnings forecasts. This effect does not hold when the CEO is powerf=
ul, overconfident, or entrenched. Acquisitions by firms without specialized=
 staff do not create value, on average. We provide evidence on mechanisms t=
hrough which specialized M&A staff improves acquisition performance. Fo=
r identification, we use the staggered recognition of inevitable disclosure=
 doctrine as a source of exogenous variation in the employment of specializ=
ed M&A staff.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So it=E2=80=
=99s a bit of both, but mostly M&A departments are good. Also, the reas=
on M&A departments are good is mostly that they are good at picking tar=
gets, not that they are great at negotiating prices =E2=80=94=C2=A0though t=
hey=C2=A0<em>do=C2=A0</em>seem to be good at keeping down the costs of inve=
stment-bank advice:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">How does sp=
ecialized M&A staff create value for the acquirer=E2=80=99s shareholder=
s? Our results suggest that specialized M&A staff helps acquirers ident=
ify targets that have higher synergies with the acquirer as reflected in hi=
gher combined announcement returns and improvements in the combined firm=E2=
=80=99s abnormal operating performance in the post-acquisition period. We d=
o not find evidence that specialized M&A staff drives a better bargain =
for the acquirer in that such acquirers capture more of the combined synerg=
y gains or pay lower takeover premiums. Moreover, while these firms do not =
seem to retain fewer external advisors, we find that they pay lower advisor=
y fees. The lower fees paid by firms with specialized M&A staff further=
 suggest that the specialized M&A staff performs some tasks that otherw=
ise would be performed by the investment bankers.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p =
